title: Reading Share Price Charts
objectives: Read the key components of a line and candlestick chart
Identify trends, support levels and resistance levels
Explain what trading volume tells you about price movements

vocabulary.definition: A chart showing closing prices over time connected by a line. The simplest view of a share's price history.
A chart showing open, high, low and close prices for each period. Each 'candle' is green (price rose) or red (price fell). More information than a line chart.
The general direction of a share price over time. Uptrend = higher highs and higher lows. Downtrend = lower highs and lower lows. Sideways = no clear direction.
A price level where a share tends to stop falling and bounce back up — because buyers step in at that price. Acts like a floor.
A price level where a share tends to stop rising and pull back — because sellers emerge at that price. Acts like a ceiling.
The number of shares traded in a period. High volume on a price move gives it more significance — many investors agreeing on the direction.

activities.title: The Line Chart — Your Starting Point
activities.notePrompt: Write: What does a line chart show? What does it NOT show? When would you want more detail than a line chart provides?
activities.content:
The line chart is the most basic way to visualise a share's price history. It plots closing prices over time and connects them with a line.

What it shows:
— The overall trend (up, down or sideways)
— Major turning points — peaks and troughs
— How today's price compares to past prices
— The general shape of performance over a period (1 week, 1 month, 1 year, 5 years)

What it does NOT show:
— How much the price moved within any single day
— Whether a day's close was near its high or low
— Trading volume (how many shares changed hands)
— Opening prices

How to read a line chart:
Look at the time axis (x-axis) — this is the date range. Look at the price axis (y-axis) — this is the price in NZD. The line itself shows how price changed across the time range. A line trending up and to the right is an uptrend. Down and to the right is a downtrend.

NZ example: Fisher and Paykel Healthcare's 5-year line chart shows a strong uptrend from 2016 to 2021, a sharp peak around $38 in early 2021, then a significant downtrend through 2022, followed by partial recovery. The chart tells this story instantly — no words needed.

For most investors, a 1-year and 5-year line chart is enough context to understand recent price behaviour.

activities.title: Candlestick Charts — More Information Per Day
activities.notePrompt: Draw a single green candlestick and a single red candlestick, labelling: open, close, high, low, body, and wick. Then explain what each element represents.
activities.content:
Candlestick charts show four prices for each period: open, high, low and close. Each candle summarises a full day (or week, or hour) of trading.

How to read a candlestick:
— The body is the thick rectangle — it spans from open to close
— Green body: close was HIGHER than open (price rose during the period)
— Red body: close was LOWER than open (price fell during the period)
— The upper wick (thin line above body): the highest price reached during the period
— The lower wick (thin line below body): the lowest price reached during the period

Example: Mainfreight, one trading day
Open: $68.00, High: $69.80, Low: $67.40, Close: $69.20
Body: green (close $69.20 > open $68.00)
Upper wick: from $69.20 to $69.80
Lower wick: from $68.00 to $67.40

What this tells us: Mainfreight opened at $68, dipped to $67.40 early in the session (testing a support level), then rallied to a high of $69.80 before settling at $69.20. Buyers were in control — the stock closed near its daily high. This is a bullish candlestick pattern.

A long lower wick with a green body is often called a 'hammer' — it shows sellers tried to push the price down but buyers overwhelmed them. These patterns matter in technical analysis.

activities.statement: A share in a strong uptrend will always have higher prices this year than last year.
activities.explanation: False. An uptrend is defined as a series of higher highs and higher lows over time — but individual weeks or months within the trend can have down periods. Even strong uptrends have pullbacks and corrections. A share can be in a long-term uptrend while also being significantly below last year's high — for example if a correction happened in the past year. Trend analysis is about the overall direction, not every single data point.

activities.title: Trends, Support and Resistance
activities.notePrompt: Draw a simple uptrend chart. Mark: two higher highs, two higher lows, a support level, and a resistance level. Then describe what might happen if the price breaks through the resistance.
activities.content:
Three concepts professional chart readers use constantly.

Trend:
Uptrend: each rally reaches a higher high than the previous rally, and each pullback only reaches a higher low than the previous pullback. Classic pattern of a strong bull run.
Downtrend: each bounce only reaches a lower high, and each fall reaches a lower low. A share in a clear downtrend is grinding lower.
Sideways trend: price oscillates between a ceiling (resistance) and a floor (support) without clear direction.

Support: A price level where the share has repeatedly stopped falling and bounced. Buyers consistently step in at this level — perhaps because they believe it is 'good value' or because stop-loss orders are clustered there. Example: Spark NZ repeatedly bouncing at $4.00 over 18 months makes $4.00 a support level.

Resistance: A price level where the share has repeatedly failed to break through. Sellers consistently emerge at this level — taking profits or establishing short positions. Example: Air New Zealand hitting $0.90 multiple times without breaking through makes $0.90 a resistance level.

Breakouts: When a share breaks through a resistance level on high volume, it often continues rising — the 'ceiling' has become a new 'floor.' This breakout pattern is one of the most commonly watched signals in technical analysis. The opposite — a break below support — is bearish.

Caveat: These are patterns and tendencies, not certainties. Technical analysis is a tool for forming probabilities, not predictions.

questions.question: A share has the following weekly closing prices: $10, $11, $10.50, $12, $11.20, $13, $12.50. What trend is this share in?
options: Downtrend — the share fell from $11 to $10.50 and from $12 to $11.20
Sideways trend — prices are oscillating without clear direction
Uptrend — the series shows higher highs ($11→$12→$13) and higher lows ($10.50→$11.20→$12.50)
No trend — the data is too short to identify a trend
questions.explanation: This is an uptrend. Despite the pullbacks (from $11 to $10.50 and from $12 to $11.20), each successive high is higher than the last ($11, $12, $13) and each successive low is higher than the last ($10.50, $11.20, $12.50). Higher highs and higher lows is the textbook definition of an uptrend. The pullbacks are normal — uptrends always include temporary retracements.

questions.question: Meridian Energy has bounced from $5.20 four times over the past year each time it touched that level. What does this make $5.20?
options: A resistance level — sellers consistently emerge at $5.20
A support level — buyers consistently step in at $5.20, preventing further falls
The fair value of Meridian shares as determined by the NZX
A price ceiling imposed by Meridian's board
questions.explanation: A price level where a share repeatedly stops falling and bounces is a support level — buyers consistently find value at that price and buy, absorbing selling pressure. $5.20 has acted as a 'floor' for Meridian multiple times. Support levels are identified by repeated bounces, not set by any authority. If Meridian were to close decisively below $5.20 on heavy volume, this former support could become new resistance.

questions.question: A share breaks above its 12-month resistance level of $8.50, closing at $8.80 on its highest trading volume in 3 months. What does this signal?
options: The share is now overvalued and should be sold immediately
A bearish signal — breaking above resistance on high volume typically means a reversal downward is coming
A bullish breakout — the prior resistance level may now become support, and the high volume confirms broad investor participation in the move
Nothing significant — price movements above resistance occur randomly and have no predictive value
questions.explanation: A breakout above a significant resistance level on high volume is one of the most watched bullish signals in technical analysis. The high volume is critical — it indicates many investors participated in the breakout, making it more likely to be sustained rather than a false alarm. The prior resistance of $8.50 may now become a support level (buyers who missed the breakout often buy pullbacks to the old resistance). This does not guarantee further gains, but the probability is considered favourable by technical analysts.

activities.title: Line Chart or Candlestick? Match the Use Case

pairs.item: Quick check of whether a share is up or down over the past year
Understanding how much price fluctuated within individual trading days
Looking for hammer and reversal patterns on specific days
Showing 5-year performance to assess long-term investment returns

activities.explanation: Line charts are better for big-picture trend and performance analysis. Candlestick charts provide more granular information about each period — open, high, low, close — making them more useful for technical pattern analysis. Most serious investors use both: line charts for context, candlestick charts for shorter-term pattern analysis.

activities.statement: High trading volume on a price rise makes the rise more significant and reliable than the same price rise on low volume.
activities.explanation: True. Volume is the market's vote of confidence. When many investors participate in a price move (high volume), it reflects broad agreement about direction. A share rising 3% on ten times its average daily volume is a stronger signal than the same 3% rise on minimal volume. Low-volume price moves can be caused by a single large order and may not reflect real market sentiment — they are more prone to reversal.

activities.question: A share's price chart shows a series: $20 high, $18 low, $22 high, $19.50 low, $24 high, $21 low. Is this an uptrend, downtrend or sideways? Type one word.
activities.hint: Check whether each successive high is higher than the last, and each successive low is higher than the last.
activities.explanation: This is an uptrend. Highs: $20 → $22 → $24 (each higher). Lows: $18 → $19.50 → $21 (each higher). Higher highs and higher lows define an uptrend. The share is making consistent progress upward despite regular pullbacks between each peak.

activities.title: Look Up a Real Chart
activities.prompt: Go to nzx.com or Google Finance and pull up the 1-year chart for any NZ company you recognise. Describe in writing: Is it in an uptrend, downtrend or sideways trend? Can you spot any obvious support or resistance levels? Did anything dramatic happen — sharp rises or falls? What do you think might have caused the biggest move you can see?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — reading real charts is the fastest way to build pattern recognition.
